title: The problem with arrays
instruction:
Arrays are great when you know the size upfront. But what if you do not?

You are building a library catalog. You have no idea how many books the library will acquire. You cannot write `[5]string` when it might be 2 books or 50.

This is exactly the problem slices solve. Run this and see what happens when you try to add a 4th book to a 3-slot array.

codeChecks.message: Change [3]string to []string to make it a slice instead of an array.

successMessage: Index out of range. The array refused. A slice would have handled that without complaint. That is why slices exist.

title: Declare an empty slice
instruction:
A slice looks almost identical to an array — but with no number in the brackets.

`var books []string` — empty slice, ready to receive titles.

An empty slice is not nil. It is just... empty. Length zero, waiting.

Run this and see what an empty slice looks like when printed.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	var books []string
	var pages []int
	fmt.Println(books)
	fmt.Println(pages)
	fmt.Println(len(books))
	fmt.Println(len(pages))
}
successMessage: Empty slices print as []. Length is 0. No panic, no error — just ready and waiting.

title: make() — create a slice with a known size
instruction:
When you know how many shelves you need upfront, use `make`.

`make([]int, 5)` creates a slice of 5 integers, all set to zero.

This is more efficient than starting empty and appending one by one.

`make([]string, 3)` creates 3 empty string slots.

Run this. Then add a line that creates a `shelves` slice of 4 ints using make and prints it.

codeChecks.message: Create a shelves slice: shelves := make([]int, 4)
Print shelves: fmt.Println(shelves)
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: make creates a slice with a set size, all values at their zero. Use it when you know the size. Use var when you do not.

title: Declare with values
instruction:
When you already have the titles, declare and fill in one shot.

`books := []string{"Dune", "1984", "Moby Dick"}`

No size number needed — Go counts the values and sets the length automatically.

Create a `authors` slice with 4 author names and print it.

codeChecks.message: Create an authors slice: authors := []string{"...", ...}
Print it: fmt.Println(authors)
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: []type{values} — no size needed. Go counts for you.

title: append — add to the end
instruction:
This is the most important slice operation.

`append(slice, newValue)` returns a new slice with the value added.

Critical rule: you must reassign the result back to the variable.

`books = append(books, "Neuromancer")` ✓
`append(books, "Neuromancer")` ✗ — the original is unchanged

Add three more books to the library using append, one at a time. Print after each.
hint: books = append(books, "Neuromancer")  then fmt.Println(books). Repeat for two more.
expectedOutput: [Dune 1984 Moby Dick Neuromancer Fahrenheit 451 Brave New World]

codeChecks.message: Append Neuromancer: books = append(books, "Neuromancer")
Append Fahrenheit 451: books = append(books, "Fahrenheit 451")
Append Brave New World: books = append(books, "Brave New World")
Replace the TODO lines with your append calls.

successMessage: append returns a new slice — always reassign. That is the one rule beginners forget.

title: len and cap
instruction:
Every slice has two numbers:

- `len(s)` — how many books are currently in the slice
- `cap(s)` — how much shelf space Go has reserved in memory

When you append and the slice runs out of capacity, Go automatically doubles the reserved space. You never manage this yourself — but understanding it explains why cap is sometimes bigger than len.

Run this and watch len and cap change as you add books.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	books := []string{}
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(books), cap(books))
	books = append(books, "Dune")
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(books), cap(books))
	books = append(books, "1984")
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(books), cap(books))
	books = append(books, "Moby Dick")
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(books), cap(books))
	books = append(books, "Neuromancer")
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(books), cap(books))
	books = append(books, "Fahrenheit 451")
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(books), cap(books))
}
successMessage: When the slice fills up, Go doubles the capacity. len is what you have. cap is what Go reserved. You use len — Go manages cap.

title: Access and update by index
instruction:
Slices use the same index syntax as arrays. Zero-indexed. Read with `s[i]`. Write with `s[i] = value`.

A patron returned a damaged book. Replace the book at index 2 with a new copy. Change it to `"The Great Gatsby"`, then print the whole collection.

codeChecks.message: Update index 2: books[2] = "The Great Gatsby"
Replace the TODO comment with your assignment.

successMessage: Reading and writing by index works exactly the same as arrays. The flexible part is the size — the access syntax is identical.

title: Loop with range
instruction:
Looping over a slice works exactly like looping over an array.

`for i, v := range books` gives you the index and value.
`for _, v := range books` gives you just the value.

Loop through the books and print each one formatted as `"Book 1: Dune"` (use the index + 1 as the number).
hint: for i, v := range books { fmt.Printf("Book %d: %s\n", i+1, v) }

codeChecks.message: Use range to loop: for i, v := range books
Use i+1 so the number starts at 1 not 0.
Replace the TODO comment with your loop.

successMessage: Same range syntax as arrays. Slices and arrays are nearly identical to work with — slices just do not have a fixed size.

title: Slicing a slice
instruction:
You can cut out a portion of a slice using `[low:high]` syntax.

`books[1:3]` gives you books at index 1 and 2 — the high end is exclusive.
`books[:2]` means from the start up to (not including) index 2.
`books[2:]` means from index 2 to the end.

Run this. Then add a line that prints just the middle three books.

codeChecks.message: Print the middle three with books[1:4] — index 1, 2, and 3.
Replace the TODO comment with your slice expression.

successMessage: Low is inclusive, high is exclusive. books[1:4] gives you indexes 1, 2, 3. That rule applies everywhere in Go.

title: copy() — the real duplicate
instruction:
Here is a trap that catches almost everyone.

`b := a` does NOT copy a slice. Both `a` and `b` point to the same data. Change one, and you change both.

`copy(dst, src)` creates a genuine separate copy.

Run this and see the trap. Then fix it by replacing `b := a` with a proper copy so the original shelf stays unchanged.
hint: b := make([]string, len(a))  then  copy(b, a)  — two lines replace the one assignment.

codeChecks.message: Create b with make: b := make([]string, len(a))
Copy a into b: copy(b, a)

successMessage: b := a shares the data. copy() separates it. This is one of the most important things to understand about slices in Go.

title: Remove an element
instruction:
Go has no built-in remove function for slices. You build it yourself.

To remove the book at index `i`, stitch the slice back together without it:

`books = append(books[:i], books[i+1:]...)`

The `...` spreads the second slice as individual arguments to append.

Remove the book at index 2 from the collection and print the result.
hint: books = append(books[:2], books[3:]...)  — this removes index 2.

codeChecks.message: Use append to remove index 2: books = append(books[:2], books[3:]...)
Replace the TODO comment with your remove operation.

successMessage: Slice up to the element, then the rest. Stitch together with append. No built-in delete needed.

title: Slice of slices
instruction:
A slice can hold other slices. Each inner slice can be a different length — unlike a 2D array which must be rectangular.

This is useful for organizing books into genre sections where each genre has a different number of books.

Run this. Then add a fourth genre section with two books.
hint: append the new genre: library = append(library, []string{"Foundation", "Hyperion"})

codeChecks.message: Append a new genre section: library = append(library, []string{"...", "..."})
Replace the TODO comment with your append.

successMessage: Slices of slices are flexible — sections can be different lengths. That is the key difference from a 2D array.

title: Book list — start
instruction:
Time to build something real.

You are managing a small library. Start by creating a slice with three books and printing the collection.

codeChecks.message: Create a books slice: books := []string{"book1", "book2", "book3"}
Print the collection: fmt.Println(books)
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: Three books. The collection exists. Now let's make it do something.

title: Book list — add more books
instruction:
The library is getting new arrivals.

Append two more books to your collection. Print the list after each addition so you can see it grow.
hint: books = append(books, "Neuromancer")  then fmt.Println(books). Repeat for a second book.

codeChecks.message: Use append to add a new book: books = append(books, "title")
Add two new books using two separate append calls.
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: 5 books now. Slices grow as your library grows. No upfront size required.

title: Book list — remove a book
instruction:
"1984" is being checked out permanently — time to remove it from the collection.

It is at index 1. Use the remove pattern to take it out and print the updated list.

codeChecks.message: Remove index 1: books = append(books[:1], books[2:]...)
Replace the TODO comment with your remove.

successMessage: Book removed from the collection. One item shorter, just like a real library system.

title: Book list — final catalog
instruction:
A new arrival just came in: "Brave New World".

Append it, then print the complete numbered catalog:
```
1. Dune
2. Moby Dick
3. Neuromancer
4. Fahrenheit 451
5. Brave New World
```
hint: books = append(books, "Brave New World")  then loop: for i, b := range books { fmt.Printf("%d. %s\n", i+1, b) }

codeChecks.message: Append the new book: books = append(books, "Brave New World")
Use range to loop through books.
Use i+1 so the numbering starts at 1.
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: A working library catalog. You created it, added to it, removed from it, added again, and printed it cleanly. That is a real feature.
